summarization.a: The figure shows how HfO₂ coverage and crystallinity change when the amount and arrangement of surface hydroxyl (OH) groups are varied. As the percentage of randomly placed OH groups increases, the crystalline part and total crystallinity increase strongly. The surface with 100% random OH gives the highest crystallinity. In contrast, OH groups placed mainly on dimers give much lower crystallinity, even though the OH amount is moderate. This shows that how OH groups are arranged on the surface is as important as how many are present.

a.question: Which surface types are compared in this figure?
a.answer:
-Random OH surfaces
, -Fully random OH surface
, -OH on dimers surface

a.question: Which surface shows the highest crystallinity?
Why does the “OH on dimers” surface show lower crystallinity than random OH surfaces?
a.answer: On the dimer surface, OH groups are clustered together instead of being spread out. This limits where growth can start and keeps reactions confined to small areas. Because of this, many regions remain disordered, and fewer large crystalline regions can form.

a.question: Why does crystallinity increase when more random OH groups are present?
a.answer: More random OH groups give more evenly spread reaction sites on the surface. This allows incoming precursor molecules to attach at many places instead of a few spots. As a result, growth becomes more uniform and connected, which helps crystalline regions form and expand.
